planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Japanese painted fern requires consistently moist soil and will wilt and lose its silvery color in dry conditions — in zone 9 summers, regular irrigation and deep organic mulch are essential.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Japanese painted fern was named the Perennial Plant of the Year in 2004 and is without question the most beautiful and most widely grown decorative fern in American horticulture — producing fronds in a distinctive, complex silver-gray, burgundy-red, and green tricolor pattern that makes it look more like an ornamental foliage plant than a typical fern. The triangular, arching fronds emerge with silver-gray upper surfaces overlaid with green and wine-red to burgundy coloring along the midrib (rachis) and frond divisions — the most colorful and decorative foliage texture of any commonly grown shade perennial. Japanese painted fern is a deciduous to semi-deciduous perennial in zone 9 that dies back in winter and re-emerges in spring. It performs best in zones 3–8 and struggles somewhat in zone 9's summer heat and humidity — it can be grown successfully in zone 9 with careful siting in deep shade, consistently moist, organic-rich soil, and protection from hot, dry winds. In zone 9, it often goes semi-dormant in summer heat and recovers in fall. Partner with hostas, astilbes, and Solomon's seal in moist shade gardens for complementary textures and colors.
sources: Perennial Plant Association — Perennial Plant of the Year 2004

common_name: Lady in Red Fern (Red-Stemmed Lady Fern)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Lady in Red is more tolerant of summer heat and drier conditions than Japanese painted fern, making it somewhat more reliable in zone 9 — though consistent moisture remains important.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Lady in Red is an exceptional and distinctive lady fern selection with bright cherry-red to deep wine-red stems (stipes) that contrast dramatically with the lacy, light-green fronds — one of the most striking color effects in shade-garden ferns. The vivid red-to-burgundy stem color persists from spring emergence through fall, providing a seasonal color effect unmatched by most other ferns. The fronds are finely divided and lacy, with the characteristic lady fern form — lighter and more delicate in texture than southern shield fern or wood fern, creating a soft, airy quality in the shade garden. Lady in Red grows larger and more vigorously than Japanese painted fern (18–30 inches vs. 12–18 inches) and is somewhat more heat-tolerant and drought-tolerant, making it more reliable in zone 9. Like most lady ferns, it is deciduous in zone 9 — dying back in late fall and re-emerging in spring — but the spring growth is vigorous. Outstanding for moist shade situations such as stream banks, bog gardens, and the deep shade of large trees. Pair with hostas, toad lilies, and Solomon's seal for layered shade-garden compositions.

common_name: Henry's Garnet Virginia Sweetspire (Fall Color Shade Shrub)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter, spring, or fall in moist, well-drained to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Henry's Garnet tolerates wet, periodically flooded soils better than most landscape shrubs — it is excellent for rain gardens and moist, low-lying areas.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Henry's Garnet is the most widely grown and most celebrated Itea virginica cultivar — a superior selection chosen by Mary Henry of Gladwyne, Pennsylvania, for its outstanding combination of fragrant summer flowers, exceptional fall color, and adaptability to difficult growing conditions. In late June and July, the shrub produces 4–6 inch, drooping, bottlebrush-like clusters of small, fragrant white flowers that attract bees and butterflies. But Henry's Garnet's most remarkable attribute is its fall color — in September through November, the foliage transitions to vivid burgundy-red to deep garnet, one of the most reliable and intense fall color displays of any native shrub, maintained even in zone 9 where fall temperatures are mild. The fall color persists for 6–8 weeks — far longer than most fall-color shrubs. Henry's Garnet spreads by suckering to form a colony over years, making it effective as a mass planting for naturalizing, rain garden planting, and stream-bank erosion control. Unlike most fall-color shrubs that require full sun for good color development, Henry's Garnet develops excellent color even in partial shade — a notable advantage for shade gardens. The spreading habit and outstanding fall color with shade tolerance make it one of the finest native shrubs for zone 9.

planting.note: Plant container vines in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; provide a trellis, fence, or arbor support — Major Wheeler is a twining vine that requires vertical support and will reach 10–15 feet at maturity.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade with vertical support; Major Wheeler is widely considered the finest cultivar of trumpet honeysuckle (Lonicera sempervirens) — the native American hummingbird vine — selected for its exceptionally prolific and long flower production. While the species is already outstanding for hummingbirds, Major Wheeler produces dramatically more flowers per season and repeats bloom flushes more reliably than unselected species plants. The 1.5–2 inch, tubular scarlet-to-coral-red flowers are produced in dense terminal clusters from spring through fall in zone 9, creating an almost unmatched hummingbird feeding resource — Ruby-throated hummingbirds visit repeatedly, and the vine at peak bloom will attract multiple hummingbirds simultaneously. Unlike the invasive Japanese honeysuckle (Lonicera japonica), trumpet honeysuckle is a well-behaved native that does not self-seed aggressively or spread invasively. The semi-evergreen foliage is attractive, with the characteristic perfoliate leaves (the vine stem appears to grow through the center of the leaf pairs) near the flowers. Major Wheeler is drought-tolerant once established and adaptable to a wide range of soils and light conditions from full sun to partial shade. The profusion of red flowers followed by small red berries provides exceptional wildlife value throughout the year.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; Stokesia requires excellent drainage and will not tolerate wet soil, particularly during winter — heavy clay soils must be amended with grit and organic matter for adequate drainage.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; Blue Danube is the classic Stoke's aster cultivar — one of the most reliable and widely planted named selections of Stokesia laevis, an attractive native wildflower of the southeastern United States ranging from South Carolina to Louisiana. The large (3–4 inch), lavender-blue to periwinkle-blue flowers have the distinctive multi-petaled, fringed structure of stokesia — each flower head resembles a very large cornflower (Centaurea) with deeply cut, fringe-edged blue petals radiating from a lighter-colored center. The clear blue to lavender-blue flower color is exceptional in the summer border and combines beautifully with yellow, white, and orange companions. Blue Danube typically blooms from June through August in zone 9, with the first flush being the heaviest. Stoke's aster is native to the pine savannas and flatwoods of the Deep South and is perfectly adapted to the heat, humidity, and sandy soils of the Gulf Coastal Plain — one of the most underused native perennials for zone 9 full-sun gardens. It is reliably perennial in zone 9, forming expanding clumps that can be divided every 3–4 years. Exceptional drought tolerance once established; deer-resistant.
